steps.title: Simplify Equation 1 to find the slope
steps.content:
Start with the first equation:
$$\frac{3}{2}y-\frac{1}{4}x=\frac{2}{3}-\frac{3}{2}y$$
Collect all $y$ terms on the left by adding $\frac{3}{2}y$ to both sides:
$$\frac{3}{2}y + \frac{3}{2}y - \frac{1}{4}x = \frac{2}{3}$$
$$3y - \frac{1}{4}x = \frac{2}{3}$$
Rearrange to isolate $y$:
$$3y = \frac{1}{4}x + \frac{2}{3}$$
$$y = \frac{1}{12}x + \frac{2}{9}$$
The slope of Equation 1 is $\frac{1}{12}$.

steps.title: Rearrange Equation 2 to slope-intercept form
steps.content:
Start with the second equation:
$$\frac{1}{2}x+\frac{3}{2}=py+\frac{9}{2}$$
Rearrange to isolate the $py$ term:
$$\frac{1}{2}x = py + \frac{9}{2} - \frac{3}{2}$$
$$\frac{1}{2}x = py + 3$$
Divide both sides by $p$ (assuming $p \neq 0$):
$$y = \frac{1}{2p}x - \frac{3}{p}$$
The slope of Equation 2 is $\frac{1}{2p}$.

steps.title: Set slopes equal and solve for p
steps.content:
A system of two linear equations has no solution when the lines are parallel (same slope) but not identical (different $y$-intercepts). Set the slopes equal:
$$\frac{1}{12} = \frac{1}{2p}$$
Cross-multiply:
$$2p = 12$$
$$p = 6$$
Verify: With $p = 6$, Equation 2 becomes $y = \frac{1}{12}x - \frac{1}{2}$. This shares the slope $\frac{1}{12}$ with Equation 1 but has a different $y$-intercept ($-\frac{1}{2} \neq \frac{2}{9}$), confirming the lines are parallel and distinct with no intersection. The answer is 6.
